parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: They just sighed and walked out of the room when I tried to explain how I felt. I sat there staring at the closed door, wondering why my words always seem to bounce right off them. It has been happening for months now, and I keep telling myself it is just a rough patch. I guess I am just tired of feeling like my voice does not matter in this space. I went to make some tea to calm down, but my hands were shaking the whole time.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really painful and exhausting. Having someone sigh and walk away when you’re trying to be open can make you feel dismissed and invisible, especially after it’s been happening for months. No wonder your hands were shaking — your body sounds like it’s carrying a lot right now.

You deserve to be heard, even if this person isn’t showing up well for you in this moment.

If it helps, a few things you could do right now:
- Put both feet on the floor and take a slow breath in for 4, out for 6, a few times.
- Sip the tea slowly and notice one or two physical details around you to help your body settle.
- Write down what you wanted to say, just for yourself, so your feelings don’t have to disappear with the conversation.
- If you have the energy later, choose a calmer moment to say something like: “When you walked out, I felt dismissed. I need us to talk without either of us leaving.”

If you want, I can help you figure out what to say to them, or just stay with you while you sort through what happened.

turns.user_message: Every time I bring up how that moment made me feel, they just sigh and change the subject like it never happened. I have started keeping a mental tally of all the times I swallowed my own thoughts just to keep the peace. It is exhausting to constantly walk on eggshells around someone who refuses to acknowledge the tension. I catch myself rehearsing conversations in my head, trying to find the perfect wording that will not trigger another shutdown. The silence in the house is getting heavier, and I do not know how to fill it anymore.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really painful and lonely. You’re trying to name something real, and instead of being met with care or even basic acknowledgment, you’re getting brushed off. No wonder you feel exhausted and like you have to edit yourself all the time.

Keeping a mental tally of what you’ve swallowed can be a sign that this has been going on for a while. And rehearsing every conversation in your head is often what people do when they’re trying to stay safe in a relationship where honest feelings don’t seem welcome. That’s a heavy way to live.

A few things stand out:

- You are not overreacting for wanting your feelings to be heard.
- The “silent” treatment of changing the subject can still be a form of dismissal, even if it’s subtle.
- Walking on eggshells is draining because it forces you to constantly manage someone else’s reactions.

If you want, a next step could be to focus less on finding the “perfect wording” and more on a clear, simple boundary, like:
- “I need you to stay with this conversation for a few minutes.”
- “It hurts when my feelings get redirected away.”
- “If now isn’t a good time, let’s pick a time to talk, but I don’t want it dropped.”

If they still won’t engage, that gives you information about the relationship—not about your worth, but about their capacity or willingness to meet you.

And if you’re feeling worn down by all this, it may help to talk to someone outside the house who can give you steady support: a trusted friend, therapist, counselor, or support line.

If you want, I can help you figure out:
1. what to say next in a calm but direct way, or  
2. how to tell whether this is emotional dismissal versus a temporary communication issue.

turns.user_message: I heard that same heavy sigh echo through the hallway today, and it completely shattered whatever patience I had left. I have spent so long bending myself into shapes that fit their comfort zone that I barely recognize my own reflection anymore. Every conversation feels like a minefield where I am the only one getting burned by the fallout. I sit in the quiet and replay every single time I backed down, wondering when I stopped fighting for my own peace. It is suffocating to live in a space where my presence is tolerated but never truly seen.
turns.model_response:
That sounds deeply exhausting and painfully lonely. Living in a place where you feel tolerated rather than seen can wear you down in a way that’s hard to put into words, especially when you’ve been shrinking yourself for so long just to keep the peace.

What you’re describing also sounds like more than just a bad day — it sounds like a pattern of carrying the emotional cost while others get to stay comfortable. No wonder that sigh hit you so hard. It probably wasn’t just the sound itself; it was everything it represents.

You deserve space where you don’t have to constantly monitor yourself, brace for impact, or apologize for existing. And it makes sense that you’re looking back and wondering when you stopped protecting your own peace — that awareness can hurt, but it can also be the first step toward reclaiming it.

If you want, we can talk through:
- what’s been happening specifically,
- how to start setting boundaries without escalating things,
- or just stay with the feeling for a moment and untangle it together.

And if you’re feeling overwhelmed in a way that makes you think you might hurt yourself or you don’t feel safe, please tell someone right away or reach out to emergency support where you are.
